checks.a_analysis: The output faithfully represents the textual content of the source document. No fabricated paragraphs or sentences were found. The section headings ('NC Parcels Project Status by County', 'Parcel Updates:', 'Sources:') and the body text (date, legend items, and source description) all have a direct basis in the source image. The image alt text and caption are AI-generated for accessibility, which is an acceptable change.
The source document does not contain any contact information (phone numbers, emails, addresses, or URLs), and none were added to the output.

The output contains all key sections of the single-page source document: the title, the date, the color-coded map (as an image), the legend, and the source information. No sections or content are missing.
The source document does not contain any tables. The legend ('Parcel Updates:') is appropriately represented as a list in the output.

checks.a_analysis: The text in the output matches the source document exactly in terms of meaning and specific data points (e.g., the specific counts and dates in the legend). No wording changes that alter meaning were found.

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the source PDF and the converted output. There are no instances where text blocks, paragraphs, or lists are duplicated in the output. Each element from the source (title, date, map legend, and sources text) appears exactly once in the structured JSON.

The output establishes a clear and logical heading hierarchy. The main document title 'NC Parcels Project Status by County' is assigned as the section title (effectively H1). The sub-sections 'Parcel Updates:' and 'Sources:' are correctly identified as level 2 headings. No heading levels are skipped.

The source document does not contain any formal tables. The map legend, which is sometimes mistaken for a table, has been correctly represented as a list, which is an appropriate structural interpretation for this content type.

The reading order in the output follows the logical top-to-bottom flow of the document: Title -> Date -> Map Image -> Legend -> Sources. This matches the visual and semantic organization of the source material.
The map legend 'Parcel Updates:' is correctly converted into an unordered list. The list items faithfully represent the text and associated data (counts) found in the source legend.
There are no links present in the source document, and none have been fabricated or injected into the output.

checks.a_analysis: The source PDF is a map-based report where the central visual and organizational element is the map of North Carolina counties. In the HTML output, the map is entirely missing, resulting in a fundamentally different visual organization. The layout has collapsed from a spatial representation with supporting text into a simple vertical list of text and logo components. This significantly changes how a user interacts with the information, as the spatial relationships between the county statuses are lost.
checks.b_evidence_source: A central map of North Carolina counties color-coded to indicate parcel update status.
checks.b_evidence_output: The map is absent; the HTML contains text headers and a bulleted list where the map should be.

checks.a_analysis: The document does not contain traditional grid tables. The legend and source information are rendered clearly in the HTML as structured lists/paragraphs.

Several critical meaningful images from the source PDF are missing in the HTML output. The most significant is the status map itself. Additionally, the compass rose (top left) and the scale bar (bottom center) are absent. While the NC GICC logo is present, the colored swatches in the legend (which are meaningful icons correlating status to the map) are also missing from the bulleted list in the HTML.
checks.b_evidence_source: Large status map of NC, compass rose icon, scale bar (0-100 miles), and colored square swatches in the 'Parcel Updates' legend.
checks.b_evidence_output: The map, compass rose, scale bar, and legend color swatches are all missing from the HTML rendering.

checks.a_analysis: There is no evidence of body content being duplicated in the HTML output. Elements like the title appearing once and the legend appearing once are consistent with the source document.
